prompt: Why do long-horizon agents need trajectory-level credit assignment and richer intermediate signals?
reference_answer: Long-horizon agents need trajectory-level credit assignment because their behavior unfolds across many dependent steps rather than a single answer. RAGEN frames agent reasoning as trajectory-level multi-turn reinforcement learning in stochastic environments, ASearcher shows that deep search can span beyond ten turns and over one hundred tool calls so outcome-only signals become sparse, and CaRR argues that binary outcomes miss comprehensiveness and factuality unless citation-aware rubric rewards are added. Together they show that long-horizon agents need richer intermediate signals than final-task success alone.

documents.title: RAGEN overview digest
documents.content: RAGEN frames interactive reasoning as trajectory-level multi-turn reinforcement learning in stochastic environments. The project treats agent reasoning as a sequential control problem instead of isolated single-turn prompting, which makes credit assignment important.

documents.title: ASearcher digest
documents.content: ASearcher says long-horizon deep search can require many turns and tool calls, sometimes beyond one hundred, which makes outcome-only supervision sparse. The project scales asynchronous RL to long-horizon search so those longer trajectories become trainable.

documents.title: CaRR digest
documents.content: CaRR argues that binary outcome rewards for deep search agents miss comprehensiveness, factuality, and grounded reasoning quality. It introduces citation-aware rubric rewards and evidence-chain checks so the reward reflects intermediate evidence quality rather than only final correctness.

required_claims.text: RAGEN treats agent reasoning as trajectory-level multi-turn reinforcement learning and a sequential control problem.
ASearcher shows that long-horizon search can run beyond ten turns and over one hundred tool calls, making outcome-only supervision sparse.
CaRR adds citation-aware rubric rewards because binary outcomes miss comprehensiveness, factuality, and grounded intermediate evidence quality.
Together these systems show that long-horizon agents need richer intermediate signals and trajectory-level credit assignment rather than final-task success alone.

provenance.refresh_hint: Refresh if these projects materially change how they describe multi-turn credit assignment or citation-aware reward shaping.
notes: Crosses reasoning-RL framing with search-agent reward design.
